topic: Someone receives a package intended for the previous tenant
raw_output:
[story 1]

The knock on the door felt like an invasion. I stared at the delivery notice taped to the mailbox—a box addressed to the previous occupant. A knot tightened in my chest, a cold, hard pressure that made my jaw clench. I didn't want it. I certainly didn't want anything from them. When I finally dragged the heavy cardboard inside, the scent of stale cigarette smoke and cheap perfume immediately assaulted my senses. I ripped open the tape with unnecessary force, my knuckles white. Inside, nestled in packing peanuts, was a collection of old, sentimental junk that felt like a deliberate insult. I shoved it toward the hallway, my breath catching in a shallow, uneven rhythm. Every instinct screamed at me to burn it, to erase the evidence of their presence.

[story 2]

It arrived on a Tuesday, a dull brown box left by the courier. When I saw the return address, the familiar, unwelcome handwriting of the former resident made my stomach turn. I didn't even bother to look closely at the contents until I was inside, staring at the mismatched mugs and faded photographs. A slow, simmering anger began to build behind my eyes, a hot, prickly sensation. I moved with deliberate slowness, every step measured, as if handling something contaminated. I didn't want to acknowledge them, didn't want to acknowledge the ghost of their tenancy lingering in these boxes. I slammed the door shut, the sound echoing in the sudden silence, and immediately started stacking the items in the darkest corner of the utility room, burying the intrusion under layers of indifference.

[story 3]

The package sat on my doorstep, an unwelcome artifact of someone else's life. I picked it up, the cardboard rough beneath my fingertips, and felt a surge of pure irritation wash over me. It was an imposition, a reminder of a time I desperately wanted to forget. My gaze swept over the contents—a stack of poorly written poetry and a tarnished silver locket. A sharp, involuntary hiss escaped my lips. I didn't open it right away. Instead, I paced the small living room, the tension coiling in my shoulders until they ached. I walked to the window, watching the street below, feeling a profound sense of resentment toward the entire situation, toward the person who had left behind this unwanted legacy. I finally dropped the box onto the floor with a thud that felt disproportionately loud.
